speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to ask you a little bit about the reunion with your mother-- the first time you saw your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came home. I didn't want to come home, for it took a long time before there was enough transportation to get us home. But I remember when all the people who lived in Amsterdam went on the track and be delivered home, I wanted to be the last one to get off the track. I didn't want to get home. And they knew I was coming. And when I came and rang the doorbell, the place was full of people-- friends and so on, and so on, and so on. And everybody kissed and hugged me. And [NON-ENGLISH] was there. My mother was there. And my fiance was there, and my sister was there. And there were just voices, and voices, and voices.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked at these faces and said, who are they-- not aloud-- who are these people? I didn't recognize the face of my fiance and his voice; my mother, my sister-- none of these people. They were strangers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't surprised, because I'd expected it. But they didn't. So there was pretense going on for a while. And they treated me like an invalid. You know, you want this and this. I mean, you know, I could have killed six people. They would have said, it's OK. I understand-- or robbed 20 banks. They would have said, it's OK. I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mom spend the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mom spend the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she spent it-- first, my fiance took care of her. And the last-- I don't even know-- six months or nine months-- she was under her-- she was under her door, because she was in hiding. My fiance had arranged this somewhere. Because she finally decided she was Hungarian-- like if somebody had cared. And she was fine until they started with the Hungarians, which was later on. So she was safe, thanks God. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when was the next time you saw your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister was there. But you mean, my twin or my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, your twin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many years later. And I didn't even recognize her. And she didn't recognize me. She said, you're my sister? You're my twin-- in London-- when I was in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you marry your fiance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I married him on the 26-- 23rd? 26th of January, 1946-- shouldn't have, either-- didn't want to, either, but didn't have the guts to tell him, listen. I don't know who you are. You're a very nice man. And he did an awful lot of good during the war. I mean, he really helped an awful lot of people. And my beloved friend, Margot, who went all the way to Belgium to get me my-- she died. She died. She was arrested helping Jews-- saving Jews-- and was sent to Auschwitz at the end of the war and died on tuberculosis in-- in the wards, in one of the hospitals. And I was in Switzerland at the time. And I couldn't get to her at the last moment. There were snowstorms, and no plane, and no car, and so on. I have her--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Eva survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: --picture of-- and I will tell you. I was in Switzerland doing an awful lot of shows, and writing, and-- and records, and all of that-- working-- working like mad, you know, not-- not to think. And I had made inquiries about, of course, Margaret, and also my beloved cousin, Ferry, in Budapest, who had bought me the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I had a friend there, also-- a German poet. And in one week, I found out he had hung himself, because old people who had survived were now fleeing Europe, like me-- couldn't stand it being in Europe anymore. And I got a telegram from the Red Cross about my cousin-- shot while trying to escape. And in the same week, Margaret died in the hospital in the wards. Where-- when you left Amsterdam, where did you go? Well, we immigrated to America. And it was very strange, because my then husband said to me, think about it. You may never have the career you have in Europe over there. The war is over. Nobody wants a acting daughter anymore from a scientist professor or anything like that. They're sick of it. While over there, I could have played. He comes from a theatre family-- from a very famous theatre family. And my doors were open to me to play in Germany, to play in Austria, to play in Switzerland-- anywhere I wanted-- the big leagues, the big parts in the theatre and on film. And I had played in Switzerland-- accidentally helped out with another artist in-- in-- in a play. And she was Austrian from the famous classic [NON-ENGLISH]. And at the dress rehearsal, she gave me a whole speech about she had never been a Nazi. I never asked her. And she was good during the war and this and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why do you keep apologizing to me? Why-- why are you-- do you think you need to do this? And she told me that she had to be a member of the Nazi party, because otherwise she couldn't have the engagement and play here and there. And I said to myself, I know she wasn't a Nazi. But if this girl already turns my stomach, how am I going to play in Berlin or any of these places with somebody else I don't know what they did during the war? And we'll have a love scene, and I will think, what did you do during the war? Who were you? I kept undressing people for years afterwards. I took off their clothes, and I put them in the camps. And who are you there? Which part did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't hear the German language anymore without feeling physically sick. And I said to my husband, I can't live here anymore. I just cannot. And he said, think about it. You'll be very disappointed. This is all what you can have. And who knows, over there, you may say, good morning. Your breakfast is served, if--

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we immigrated to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in America did you immigrate to?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York. New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: First New York. And he was, of course, right, because I came, as always, too late. La Guardia-- I had started writing a book. No, they wanted me to write after the war. Publishers came to me long before Anne Frank. And I said, I can't-- out of the question. I'm much too close to it. All I can write is hate, hate, hate, hate, hate, hate. I have nothing else to say. And then I said, I will write, but I want La Guardia, the mayor, to write the introduction. That will make sense. I came to the United States, to New York, just as he died. So the notes I had, I put into a drawer and forgot about it. And then I had to make a living, which I found out is very, very difficult-- was very difficult, is very difficult, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the things you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, I got to work with the Voice of America, and that was fun. I did a lot of work, but it just didn't pay enough. Apart from doing weekend things and, you know, Purim, and bar mitzvahs, and weddings, and all this kind of-- of stupid things, and working in the eastern or the Catskills-- those are the Jewish hotels in summer. I was a hostess in a restaurant on-- on Penn Station or Grand Central Station-- got immediately pneumonia, because the air condition was hitting me like mad. Then, I-- I-- I modelled furs in August on 14th Street. It's crummy-- in a window, went around, around-- no air condition in that store-- awful. Then I worked in cheap joints across weekends in-- in-- in-- in New Jersey. And then I worked in a strip joint in New York City. I didn't strip. I was the class act there. I sang French chansons. They wanted one class act. Everybody else stripped-- right off Broadway. And then I saw an ad in the paper, which said they were looking for directors for hometown talent shows all over the United States-- put on shows-- local shows. Let us say for the school, they need a new roof, or send some children to orphanage, whatever. Half goes to the company, and half goes to the people in this town. And I wanted to see the United States. And I couldn't afford to travel. And I didn't want to see Chicago, Detroit, you know. I wanted to see the little towns. I thought it would be fun. So I saw every little town in this country from North Carolina, South Carolina, the middle west, and so on-- very educational-- very hard work. But after a while, impossible-- impossible. I mean, all small towns are alike. But in South Carolina, I saw for the very first time the difference-- black and white-- on the train station, and the toilets-- different toilets for black and white. And I must tell you, this was kind of-- quite a shock for me-- quite a shock to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- I did this for-- for-- for a long time. Then I got married. And you see, I thought-- I divorced my husband very soon. We've always been good friends, but I divorced him. I couldn't be married to anyone at that time. I mean, it was silly even to think I could. I sat alone in a rooming house for a few months on 84th Street, in New York City. And no, I was not quite there. I needed to get over a lot of things myself-- myself. And then one day, I just walked on the streets of New York, and you know, got into-- into life. I married. And I got-- I thought if I marry an American-- American roots, American family, American children-- I'll be American. I will find roots-- a home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should have not married him to begin with. [LAUGHS] But I was married about 9 or 10 years--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the children. I was never married to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and when did you come to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to Los Angeles, because my-- the little girl-- the adopted girl, which never knew another mother-- became a high fashion model at very early, in New York City, and did television, and a hit Broadway show, at the age of seven already. And then we were called out to the coast from the agency for some TV things here. So that's how we got out here. I hated it instantly, and she loved it instantly. Then she got a contract here for a television series. So that's how I got out to-- to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the first time in '59, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yeah, '57-- '59 or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one more question--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you before we go into the pictures. Did you ever go back to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? No. They didn't want me. I don't want them. I have nothing to do in Vienna-- absolutely not. They took my Austrian passport away and gave me a German one. Then they took the German away and made me an undesirable whatever-- either Austrian or German. I don't know which was better. After the war, they wanted to give me the Austrian passport back, and I said, shove it. I don't want it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I want to thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go to the pictures. I just wanted to ask you the names of your second husband and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Donald Martin.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and his two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam is the younger one, and Elaine is the adopted one-- adopted by him and his first wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much. We're going to go on the pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my mother. And this is my sister, Kate. And she's holding my sister, Ellie, and me. I'm facing to the left, and she's on the right. And this was taken in Vienna in our home, or outside our home, in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I don't know. I guess I must have been here about-- how old would you say I am? A year? Two years? So probably 1919, 1920-- I don't know how old I was then. 1919? 19-- 1919-- something like that. On the left is my father, Mr. Berthold Grohs. And next to him is his brother, Uncle Rudy. And that was taken in Vienna, too-- I think in Stadtpark, probably. I don't know the year. I think it's 1920, in Vienna, Austria. This is a class picture in high school, from my class. And there, second on the right in the second row to the end, is my twin sister, Ellie. And in the first row on the left to the end is I-- is I-- is me-- also, in Vienna, of course-- in Vienna. I don't know what grade it was. I would say I was about 14 years old and--

speaker: interviewer
text: A date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This here is a little picture of the prima ballerina, Sylvia, taken at home-- I don't know-- living room or dining room. I was about 14 or 15 years old-- toe shoes, ballet dress, before a dance recital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 14 years old. It must have been 20-- 32-- 1932, something like that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the famous theatre-- the Hollandsche Schouwburg, later on, Jewish theatre in Amsterdam, Holland, where I played, and where I did, as an actress, have the most joyous and the most devastating times of my life-- the famous Hollandsche Schouwburg-- one of the oldest theatres in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1941 or '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my beloved second cousin, Ferry, and me, taken in Budapest, Hungary in summer 19-- I would say 1936, 1937-- on the Danube, on a picnic. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a very important document. It is the ID-- the Jewish ID-- given to us by the German, to every Jewish woman. What not every Jewish woman had on there is this particular stamp. It was really worth a king's ransom. It says very clearly, temporary exempt from labor transport. So it was a safety net. And this was given to me, this stamp, in 1942, so was the ID-- in Amsterdam, in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is from a cabaret show, which I did after the war in Lucerne, Switzerland, in a lovely, lovely show. And this was in 1946. It was in the very first months of 1946-- I would say, March or April-- again, Lucerne, Switzerland, during a performance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the wedding picture between my first husband, Herbert Nelson, and me, on the 23rd of January, 1946, in Amsterdam, Holland. And we really didn't mean to look in opposite direction. It just happened that people were talking to us in opposite direction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is back to my beloved Amsterdam, in 1966, taken in front of the house I lived in on one of the wonderful [NON-ENGLISH]. The house with the flowers on the top floor-- this is where I was. And down to the left, there is me, Kate in the middle, and my twin sister at the end, with Romeo, my Australian poodle. I went back for the first time to Amsterdam to see what happened to my theatre-- what had become of it-- and the people I used to know then.
